= The Js_of_ocaml library

This page provides an overview of the Js_of_ocaml type system for representing
JavaScript values in OCaml. For practical examples, see the
<<a_manual chapter="bindings"|bindings guide>>.

== Core concept: <<a_api subproject="js_of_ocaml"|type Js_of_ocaml.Js.t>>

All JavaScript values in OCaml are wrapped in the <<a_api subproject="js_of_ocaml"|type Js_of_ocaml.Js.t>> type. This phantom
type ensures type safety when interacting with JavaScript.

{{{
'a Js.t    (* An OCaml type representing a JavaScript value *)
}}}

== Type mappings

OCaml and JavaScript represent values differently. The table below shows
the correspondence:

|= OCaml type |= Js_of_ocaml type |= JavaScript type |
| {{{int}}} | {{{int}}} (direct) | Number |
| {{{float}}} | {{{Js.number Js.t}}} | Number |
| {{{bool}}} | {{{bool Js.t}}} | Boolean |
| {{{string}}} | {{{Js.js_string Js.t}}} | String |
| {{{array}}} | {{{Js.js_array Js.t}}} | Array |

Integers can be used directly. Other types require conversion functions
like <<a_api subproject="js_of_ocaml"|val Js_of_ocaml.Js.string>>, <<a_api subproject="js_of_ocaml"|val Js_of_ocaml.Js.bool>>, etc. See
<<a_manual chapter="bindings"|Type conversions>> for details and examples.

== JavaScript objects

JavaScript objects are represented using OCaml's object types:

{{{
< field1 : type1; field2 : type2; ... > Js.t
}}}

Each "method" in the OCaml type represents a JavaScript property or method.
The property type determines access:

|= Type |= Meaning |
| {{{t Js.readonly_prop}}} | Read-only property |
| {{{t Js.writeonly_prop}}} | Write-only property |
| {{{t Js.prop}}} | Read/write property |
| {{{t1 -> ... -> tn -> t Js.meth}}} | Method |

See <<a_manual chapter="bindings"|Binding a JS object>> for examples.

== Nullable and optional values

JavaScript has {{{null}}} and {{{undefined}}}. Js_of_ocaml provides:

* <<a_api subproject="js_of_ocaml"|module Js_of_ocaml.Js.Opt>> - For values that may be {{{null}}}
* <<a_api subproject="js_of_ocaml"|module Js_of_ocaml.Js.Optdef>> - For values that may be {{{undefined}}}

See <<a_manual chapter="bindings"|Handling null and undefined>> for usage.

== Functions

OCaml and JavaScript have different calling conventions (partial application
vs. undefined for missing args). Use:

* <<a_api subproject="js_of_ocaml"|val Js_of_ocaml.Js.wrap_callback>> - Wrap OCaml functions for JavaScript
* <<a_api subproject="js_of_ocaml"|val Js_of_ocaml.Js.Unsafe.fun_call>> - Call JavaScript functions from OCaml

See <<a_manual chapter="bindings"|Calling JS functions>> and
<<a_manual chapter="bindings"|Passing OCaml functions to JavaScript>>.

== Syntax extension

The <<a_manual chapter="ppx"|PPX syntax extension>> provides convenient syntax:

{{{
obj##.prop          (* Get property *)
obj##.prop := v     (* Set property *)
obj##meth arg       (* Call method *)
new%js constr args  (* Call constructor *)
object%js ... end   (* Create object literal *)
}}}

== JSON serialization

The <<a_api subproject="js_of_ocaml"|module Js_of_ocaml.Json>> module provides
marshaling between OCaml values and JSON strings:

<<code language="ocaml"|
(* OCaml value -> JSON string *)
let json : Js.js_string Js.t = Json.output value

(* JSON string -> OCaml value (unsafe, like Marshal) *)
let value : 'a = Json.unsafe_input json
>>

For type-safe JSON handling, use <<a_manual chapter="ppx-deriving"|ppx_deriving_json>>.

== API reference

* <<a_api subproject="js_of_ocaml"|module Js_of_ocaml.Js>> - Core JavaScript bindings
* <<a_api subproject="js_of_ocaml"|module Js_of_ocaml.Dom>> - DOM bindings
* <<a_api subproject="js_of_ocaml"|module Js_of_ocaml.Dom_html>> - HTML DOM bindings
* <<a_api subproject="js_of_ocaml"|index>> - Full API index
